summary: French pioneer of science fiction, known for his series Voyages Extraordinaires. Major works include Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea (Captain Nemo), Around the World in Eighty Days (Phileas Fogg and Passepartout), Journey to the Center of the Earth (the Lidenbrocks), and From the Earth to the Moon (Baltimore Gun Club). Often targeted adolescent readers. Born 1828, died 1905.

works.name: Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea
works.description: One of Verne's most famous novels, featuring the mysterious Captain Nemo (whose real name is Prince Dakkar) aboard the submarine Nautilus. Nemo reappears in The Mysterious Island. Wikipedia

examples: One possibility is Captain Nemo, the protagonist of this author's Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea.
This speculative fiction author of Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea detailed Phileas Fogg's journeys.

works.name: Around the World in Eighty Days
works.description: Novel in which the methodical Englishman Phileas Fogg wagers he can circumnavigate the globe in 80 days, accompanied by his valet Passepartout. He saves the Indian princess Aouda. Detective Fix pursues him believing he robbed the Bank of England. Fogg wins after realizing he gained a day traveling east. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Phileas Fogg and his valet Passepartout travel around the world in 80 days
examples: This speculative fiction author of Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea detailed Phileas Fogg's journeys with Passepartout in Around the World in Eighty Days.
Phileas Fogg is the protagonist of Around the World in Eighty Days, a novel by this French author.

clues.clue: Fogg saves the Indian princess Aouda and eventually marries her
examples: This man eventually marries the Indian princess Aouda, whom he had saved from the custom of burning herself on a funeral pyre for her first husband.
Phileas Fogg... eventually marries the Indian princess Aouda.

clues.clue: Detective/Inspector Fix pursues Fogg believing he robbed the Bank of England; drugs Passepartout in an opium den; gets punched by Fogg
examples: This detective chases after Phileas Fogg, believing that Fogg recently robbed the Bank of England.

This stupid detective believes Fogg robbed the Bank of England and follows him around the world, hoping to arrest him. He drugs Passepartout in an opium den and gets punched by Fogg near the end of the novel.

clues.clue: Fogg wins his bet by realizing he returned to the Reform Club a day earlier than he thought (gained a day traveling east)
examples: Name this man who wins a bet after realizing that he has returned to the Reform Club a day earlier than he thought.
Nellie Bly visited Jules Verne in Amiens during her around-the-world trip; her race inspired by Fogg's journey.

clues.clue: Fogg fires valet James Foster for bringing shaving water at 84 degrees instead of 86 degrees Fahrenheit
examples: This character likes his shaving water at 86 degrees Fahrenheit and fires valet James Foster for bringing him water at 84 degrees.

works.name: Journey to the Center of the Earth
works.description: A message from Arne Saknussemm is discovered by Axel and his uncle Otto Lidenbrock, directing them to descend into an Icelandic glacier. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A message from Arne Saknussemm is discovered by Axel and Otto Lidenbrock, directing them to descend into an Icelandic glacier
examples: This author also wrote a novel in which a message from Arne Saknussemm is discovered by Axel and Otto Lidenbrock, telling them to descend into an Icelandic glacier.
Melies's films A Trip to the Moon and The Impossible Voyage were inspired by this French science-fiction author of From the Earth to the Moon and Journey to the Center of the Earth.

works.name: From the Earth to the Moon
works.description: The Baltimore Gun Club builds a giant cannon (the Columbiad) at Stone's Hill, Florida, to shoot themselves to the moon. Inspired the Georges Méliès film A Trip to the Moon. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The Baltimore Gun Club builds a giant rifle/cannon (the Columbiad) to shoot themselves to the moon
examples: In a novel by this author, the Baltimore Gun Club builds a giant rifle to shoot themselves to the moon.
The Baltimore Gun Club selects the fictional 'Stone's Hill' in this state [Florida] as the location of the Columbiad cannon in another novel by the same author.

clues.clue: Inspired the Georges Méliès silent film A Trip to the Moon (and also The Impossible Voyage)
examples: Melies's films A Trip to the Moon and The Impossible Voyage were inspired by this French science-fiction author of From the Earth to the Moon.
A much earlier film inspired by this author used stop-motion to depict a rocket hitting the eye of the Man in the Moon.

clues.clue: Set partly in Florida; Verne had never been to the American South
examples: Although he had never been to the American South, this French author set his novels Texar's Revenge and From the Earth to the Moon in Florida.

works.name: The Mysterious Island
works.description: Cyrus Smith and four other Americans escape a POW camp in a hot air balloon and end up on a South Pacific island that turns out to be the hideout of Captain Nemo (real name Prince Dakkar). A sequel to Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea. One of Poe's hoaxes also inspired this novel. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Cyrus Smith and four other Americans are stranded on an island that turns out to be Captain Nemo's hideout; Nemo's real name is Prince Dakkar
examples: Cyrus Smith and four other Americans are stranded on an island which turns out to be the hideout of a man whose real name is Prince Dakkar.
One of Poe's hoaxes also inspired The Mysterious Island, a novel by this author of Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea.

clues.clue: Protagonists escape a POW camp in a hot air balloon and somehow arrive at a South Pacific island
examples: In this novel, the protagonists somehow wind up at the title South Pacific locale after escaping a POW camp in a hot air balloon.

works.name: An Antarctic Mystery (The Sphinx of the Ice Fields)
works.description: Narrated by Jeorling, who returns to America from the Kerguelen Islands aboard the Halbrane. A sequel to Poe's The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym. Jeorling finds Pym's body at the magnetically charged Ice Sphinx. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Narrated by Jeorling aboard the Halbrane; a sequel to Poe's Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym; Jeorling finds Pym's body at the Ice Sphinx
examples: Another of his nautical novels is narrated by Jeorling, who is returning to America from the Kerguelen Islands aboard the Halbrane. That novel is a sort of sequel to Poe's Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym.
Jeorling finds this character's body at the magnetically charged Ice Sphinx in An Antarctic Mystery, which is a response to a novel titled for this character.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Verne is identified in quizbowl. He is a French pioneer of science fiction (or speculative fiction), closely associated with the Voyages Extraordinaires series. He targeted adolescent readers. Czech filmmaker Karel Zeman adapted his works. He was visited by Nellie Bly during her record-breaking trip. He lived in Amiens.

clues.clue: French pioneer of science fiction / speculative fiction
examples: name this pioneer of science fiction who wrote Journey to the Center of the Earth.
This French science-fiction author of From the Earth to the Moon and Journey to the Center of the Earth.

clues.clue: Author of the Voyages Extraordinaires series; Five Weeks in a Balloon is one entry
examples: Adolescent readers were a major audience for this author, whose series of Voyages Extraordinaires include Five Weeks in a Balloon and From the Earth to the Moon.

clues.clue: Wrote a novel in which a 15-year-old boy becomes captain of the Pilgrim after the crew is killed on a whale hunt
examples: This author wrote a novel in which a fifteen-year-old boy becomes the captain of the Pilgrim after the entire crew is killed on a whale hunt.

clues.clue: Nellie Bly visited Verne in Amiens during her 72-day around-the-world trip
examples: Another work about this event described a writer living in Amiens. Bly visited Jules Verne in Amiens during her trip.

clues.clue: Karel Zeman's Czech film Invention for Destruction was inspired by Verne
examples: Czech animator Karel Zeman's film Invention for Destruction was inspired by a novel by this author.

comprehensive_summary: Jules Verne (1828–1905) was a French author widely recognized as a pioneer of science fiction, best known for his prolific series Voyages Extraordinaires. In quizbowl, he is consistently identified through his major works: Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea, featuring the enigmatic Captain Nemo (revealed in The Mysterious Island as Prince Dakkar) and his submarine the Nautilus; Around the World in Eighty Days, in which the impeccably precise Phileas Fogg travels the globe with his valet Passepartout, rescues the Indian princess Aouda, and outwits Detective (Inspector) Fix while winning his bet at the Reform Club; Journey to the Center of the Earth, in which Axel and Professor Otto Lidenbrock follow a cryptic message from Arne Saknussemm into an Icelandic volcano; and From the Earth to the Moon, in which the Baltimore Gun Club fires a projectile to the moon from a cannon called the Columbiad, located at Stone's Hill in Florida. The Mysterious Island serves as a sequel to both Twenty Thousand Leagues and (by inspiration) to Edgar Allan Poe's work — Verne also wrote An Antarctic Mystery (The Sphinx of the Ice Fields), a direct sequel to Poe's Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym, narrated by Jeorling aboard the ship Halbrane. Verne's connection to film is frequently clued: Georges Méliès made A Trip to the Moon and The Impossible Voyage inspired by his works, and Czech filmmaker Karel Zeman's Invention for Destruction adapted his novels. Journalist Nellie Bly visited Verne in Amiens during her 1889 record-breaking circumnavigation. Verne's works were primarily aimed at adolescent readers, and he set several novels in the United States (Florida in particular) despite never visiting the American South.

recursive_suggestions: Edgar Allan Poe — Verne wrote An Antarctic Mystery as a sequel to Poe's Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym; mentioned several times in connection with Verne
Georges Méliès — his films A Trip to the Moon and The Impossible Voyage were both directly inspired by Verne
Nellie Bly — visited Verne in Amiens during her 1889 circumnavigation, which was inspired by Around the World in Eighty Days
Captain Nemo — appears as its own quizbowl topic; Nemo appears in both Twenty Thousand Leagues and The Mysterious Island
